theme: Find the tipping point from below it
title: Below the Tipping Point — recovering a hidden percolation scaling law
strategies: trivial: predict a flat constant equal to the mean of the sub-critical census -> reproduces the checker baseline ~0.1

greedy: ignore the stated scaling ansatz and just fit a smooth polynomial (cubic) least-squares curve through the sub-critical (p, S) census, extrapolating it directly -- it tracks the slowly-rising sub-critical data closely, so a practitioner stops there, but a smooth polynomial has no threshold and no singular exponent baked in, so on the held-out near-and-above-threshold points it either flattens out or runs away, landing far from the truth on the cases where the transition is sharp

strong: take the given scaling ansatz S(p)=A*(W*g((p-p_c)/W))^beta seriously -- grid-search the unknown critical probability p_c and exponent beta (the crossover width W is given), fit the amplitude A in closed form for each candidate via least squares, cross-check with local refinement, and emit the resulting closed-form critical law -- it carries the actual singularity so it extrapolates through and beyond the threshold

headroom above strong: refine p_c/beta with a finer local optimizer, robustly downweight the noisiest deep sub-critical rows, or trade a smaller (fewer-node) law against accuracy to beat the parsimony tax
